coredata.dc:title: High SERS performance of functionalized carbon dots in the detection of dye contaminants
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Cairo University.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Cairo University.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yanqiu
Kong, Lingru
Ding, Yong
Xia, Lixin
Cao, Shuo
Song, Peng
coredata.dc:description:
Introduction The long-term overuse of malachite green (MG) has potential carcinogenic, teratogenic, and mutagenic effects. The functional nanocomposite is novel and challenging to construct and implement through surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) strategy to reveal the contributions in application.
Objectives The novel Ag-CDs (carbon dots)-PBA (phenyl boric acid) nanocomposite was constructed by a facile route to detect toxic MG molecule with high SERS sensitivity and good uniformity.
Methods The enhanced substrate used for the detection of MG has been successfully constructed using PBA modulated Ag-CDs on a structured surface with rich binding sites.
Results The fabricated Ag-CDs-PBA substrate can be used to analyze various probe molecules exhibiting high sensitivity, good signal reproducibility, and excellent stability. The mechanism between components has been proved by calculations originating from the plasmonic Ag and active electronic transmission among the bridging CDs and PBA via the close spatial π-π effect. In addition, the accelerated separation of electron-hole pairs was triggered to further improve the SERS activity of the hybrid via a bidirectional charge transfer (CT) process. Significantly, the Ag-CDs-PBA system shows distinctive selectivity, in which PBA can hinder the interference of other species without specific hydroxyl groups.
Conclusion Based on this deeper insight on plasmon-mediated mechanism, the SERS substrate was successfully practiced for quantitative determination in real water and fish samples. The strategy developed promises to be a new sensor technology and has great potential for environmental and food safety applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SERS enhancement
Carbon dots
Charge transfer
MG detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: SERS detection of MG in fish by Ag-CDs-PBA substrate
Collection and processing of actual water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM images (a–f) with their corresponding size distributions of the Ag-CDs observed at different magnifications.

(a) XRD patterns obtained for Ag-CDs and Ag-CDs-PBA. (b) FTIR spectrum obtained for the CDs, which contain abundant surface functional groups. XPS spectra obtained for Ag-CDs and Ag-CDs-PBA: (c) Full survey, (d) Ag 3d, (e) C 1s, (f) N 1s, and (g) O 1s spectra.

Raman spectra obtained for the CDs (a), Ag-CDs (b), and Ag-CDs-PBA (c). SERS spectra obtained for MG (10−6 M) on the Ag-CDs and Ag-CDs-PBA, respectively (d). SERS spectra obtained for different concentrations of MG (10−4–10−10 M) on the Ag-CDs-PBA nanohybrid (e). Quantitative relationship curve for the logarithm of the integrated SERS band observed at 1617 cm−1 (f).

(a) SERS signal reproducibility of MG (1 × 10−5 M) on the Ag-CDs-PBA nanohybrid. (b) RSD calculation with the plot of the Raman peaks observed at 1617, 1173, and 801 cm−1 vs the different points. The Ag-CDs-PBA for applied detection. (c) The SERS spectra of R6G (concentration from 10–10 to 10–5 M). (d) The SERS spectra of CV (concentration from 10–8 to 10–5 M). (e) The SERS spectra of RB (concentration from 10–9 to 10–5 M). (f) The SERS spectra of MG (10–6 M, orange line), MB + MG (10–6 M, green line), R6G + MG (10–6 M, black line), RB + MG (10–6 M, purple line), CV + MG (10–6 M, purple red line) and their mixture (blue line) detected on the Ag-CDs-PBA structure. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Charge density difference (purple: electrons; blue: holes) and (b) transition density matrix diagram of the CDs-PBA in absorption state S30. (c) AEF calculated in comparison SERS spectra of 10−10 M MG on Ag-CDs-PBA and Raman spectra of 10−2 M MG. (d) SERS spectra of MG obtained from fish sample, the inset of (d) was the optical picture of the fish immersing in MG solution. (e) SERS spectra of MG before and after self-cleaning of Ag-CDs-PBA substrate, and (f) corresponding Raman intensities at 1617 cm−1 of 10−5 M MG when the SERS substrate is cycled for five times. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Plots of intensities at 1173 cm−1 versus different concentrations of MG solutions. (b) Plot of the linear relationship between logarithmic the intensity at 1173 cm−1 and different concentrations of MG solutions. (c) The SERS spectra of MG in river water (concentration from a to c is 10, 100 and 10000 nM) on the Ag-CDs-PBA structure. (d) The SERS spectra of MG in industrial wastewater (concentration from a to c is 10, 100 and 10000 nM) on the Ag-CDs-PBA structure.
Comparison of Ag-CDs-PBA and previously reported SERS substrates used for MG detection.

row.entry: Composite structure based on gold-nanoparticle layer and hyperbolic metamaterial

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of MG in actual water samples via the SERS method.

head.ce:title: High SERS performance of functionalized carbon dots in the detection of dye contaminants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yingkou Institute of Technology, Yingkou 115014, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yingkou Institute of Technology, Yingkou 115014, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The long-term overuse of malachite green (MG) has potential carcinogenic, teratogenic, and mutagenic effects. The functional nanocomposite is novel and challenging to construct and implement through surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) strategy to reveal the contributions in application.
The novel Ag-CDs (carbon dots)-PBA (phenyl boric acid) nanocomposite was constructed by a facile route to detect toxic MG molecule with high SERS sensitivity and good uniformity.
The enhanced substrate used for the detection of MG has been successfully constructed using PBA modulated Ag-CDs on a structured surface with rich binding sites.

The fabricated Ag-CDs-PBA substrate can be used to analyze various probe molecules exhibiting high sensitivity, good signal reproducibility, and excellent stability. The mechanism between components has been proved by calculations originating from the plasmonic Ag and active electronic transmission among the bridging CDs and PBA via the close spatial π-π effect. In addition, the accelerated separation of electron-hole pairs was triggered to further improve the SERS activity of the hybrid via a bidirectional charge transfer (CT) process. Significantly, the Ag-CDs-PBA system shows distinctive selectivity, in which PBA can hinder the interference of other species without specific hydroxyl groups.

Based on this deeper insight on plasmon-mediated mechanism, the SERS substrate was successfully practiced for quantitative determination in real water and fish samples. The strategy developed promises to be a new sensor technology and has great potential for environmental and food safety applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Dye molecules play a vital role in everyday life, but environmental pollution cannot be ignored while bringing commercial value. MG is a toxic triphenylmethyl derivative, it serves as an effective fungicide for treating parasites, fungi, and bacterial infections in fish or fish eggs, while providing dye function for wool and leather [1]. However, countries exploiting the field of pollution-free aquaculture have explicitly prohibited the use of MG, in which the minimum required performance limit (MRPL) according to the provisions of EU Act 2002/675/EC is set at 1–2 µg kg−1 [2,3]. As a specific drug used for the treatment of water mildew in fish and fish eggs, there are no cheap and effective substitutes available on the market to date, so the use of MG in aquaculture has not been completely prohibited. Consequently, the recommended dosage is typically minimal owing to its high toxicity, substantial residue, and associated side effects. Furthermore, a sensitive and selective technique is urgently required for identifying MG and its implementation toward its trace detection in a complex biological environment.

A variety of techniques have been employed for the detection of MG, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4], capillary electrophoresis Raman spectroscopy (CE-RS) [5], flow injection analysis (FIA) [6], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [7], and spectrophotometry [8]. These methods exhibit highly precise and sensitive performance, but are accompanied by cumbersome sample pre-treatment processes that are time consuming and expensive, making them difficult to apply toward the analysis of actual samples in the field. Therefore, alternative means of testing are needed that are reliable, fast, and highly sensitive. Some techniques rely on plasmon-mediated chemical reactions (PMCRs) [9–11]. For molecular identification, the amplification of electromagnetic fields on metallic plasmonic structures is mainly used to obtain spectral information on the corresponding analytes, for example infrared, plasmon-enhanced Raman [12,13] and fluorescence spectroscopy. The outstanding advantage of surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) among these spectroscopic detection techniques is that it is an ultra-sensitive analytical method, in which the Raman signal is boosted by a factor of 1010–1011 at the single molecule level [14,15]. In addition, the stability, storage, and labelling ability of the excited SERS active molecules and tags is much stronger when compared to fluorescent analytes as a promising method for fingerprinting [16,17]. Recently, various SERS technologies have been developed in pharmaceutical, analytical chemistry, and environmental monitoring and in the field of food safety and forestry in real applications [18–20]. Specifically, the Raman signal enhancement is caused by large area SERS active aggregates with densely decorated homogenetic structures that generate plasmonic hotspots [21,22]. In addition, some practical factors should be considered for a favorable SERS substrate, such as the cost, complexity of construction, and reproducibility to meet the requirements of their applications. Some nanostructured SERS active aggregates have been reported via numerous sophisticated techniques, but they are still weaker than traditional noble metal aggregates in terms of their SERS performance. Au, Ag, and Cu with distinctive plasmonic structures can facilitate the redistribution of photo, electron, and heat energy, thereby driving catalytic reactions through enhanced electromagnetic near-field or excited carriers [23,24]. The concentration of detected molecules is <10−5 mol/L [25], but some properties, such as their stability, still need to be optimized on precious metal substrates. The introduction of non-metallic materials and an improvement in the enhancement performance can meet the needs of substrate development for ultra-sensitive detection. Furthermore, most SERS substances easily act with complicated surface species upon contact and the detection of the target molecules is a challenge. It is critical to develop a highly Raman-active surface to eliminate the interference of mixed species, which shows a high coincidence and specificity between substrate components as a detecting platform.

The CDs can be applied to photocatalysis, fluorescent inks, medical diagnosis, chemicals sensor, and energy sources [26–28] because of their unique capabilities, including stable photoluminescence, water solubility, and excellent biocompatibility [29,30]. Furthermore, CDs offer functional groups, including π-systems, COOH, OH, and NH, serving as multiple binding and active sites that enhance catalytic reactions [31–33] on the surface, leading to fluorescence quenching. In this work, the π-π stacking structure of Ag-CDs-PBA was constructed using a facile one-step immersion method. For the synergistic structure, this unique valence shell electronic configuration forms a close spatial orientation with π-π interactions formed between the boronic acid groups of PBA and amine groups of CDs, and electron or energy transfer effectively quenches the fluorescence. The strong CT effect between CDs and PBA has been proved using DFT calculations. Upon the incorporation of plasmonic Ag, additional quenching of fluorescence inhibits the electrons from the CDs from reverting to the ground state. As a result, PBA modulation of the unique CDs complexed with Ag was used to exploit their typical SERS properties in the electromagnetic enhancement induced by Ag as well as the charge transfer (CT) effect between components that can drive the catalytic reaction in the photoexcited region demonstrating high sensitivity. The remarkable SERS enhancement can be attributed to not only the presence of a large number of photoelectrons through the direct band gap excitation process, and the involvement of hot carriers due to the strong plasmon resonance effects that are excited. The Ag-CDs-PBA system has excellent selectivity and its weak binding affinity by PBA can be attributed to the lack of specific hydroxyl groups [34] precisely monitoring the target molecule instantaneously. By employing the SERS strategy, the intrinsic mechanism can be elucidated with precision, focusing on energy resolution and subtle alterations occurring during the reaction process at the surface of Ag-CDs-PBA. Moreover, this significantly enhances the separation of electron-hole pairs generated by the CT process, thereby further optimizing the SERS activity of the hybrid. A deeper insight into the mechanism has been proposed and the plasmonic π-π interactions provide a new strategy for developing inexpensive, non-interference, highly sensitive, and flexible target detection in practical applications.

All chemicals were of analytical grade. Commercially available citric acid and ethylenediamine were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. MG dye was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. PBA was supplied from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). De-ionized (DI) water was freshly prepared from Milli-Q purified water (>18 MΩ cm−1) and used to prepare the dye solution and synthesis steps.

The precursor CDs of the structured SERS substrate were prepared using a hydrodynamic method, followed by dialysis [35]. The CDs were formed by condensing citric acid and ethylenediamine. The resulting polymer-like CDs were carbonized to obtain the CDs. Then, PBA powder was prepared into a 1 mM solution. The as-synthesized CDs powder (10 mg) was immerged into the prepared PBA solution. Finally, reduced AgNO3 (18 mg) by sodium citrate to further modify the CDs-PBA complex, and the required Ag-CDs-PBA substrate was obtained via a further immersion method. The Ag-CDs complex was used as a reference and the two compounds were first directly modified.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS detection of MG in fish by Ag-CDs-PBA substrate
ce:section.ce:para: First, the live fish was bought from restaurant on campus (Shenyang, China). Then, the live fish was placed in water with MG (10−4–10−7 M) for two days. Next, the fish sample (about 1.5 g) was mixed and acetonitrile (12 mL), and sonicated for 15 min, then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 2 min and this centrifugation process was repeated three times. Finally, the extract fish samples were detected by Ag-CDs-PBA substrate.

All experiments involving animals were conducted according to the ethical policies and procedures approved by the ethics committee of the Faculty of Pharmacy, Cairo University, Egypt (Approval no. MI-2364).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Collection and processing of actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In the actual Raman test, the river water used was collected in Hunhe River, Shenyang, Liaoning, China. Industrial wastewater is collected at Liaoshen Industrial Group Co., Ltd., Shenyang, Liaoning, China. Firstly, the actual water samples collected were treated, and the solid impurities in river water and industrial wastewater were filtered out by 0.45 μm filter membrane, and then 1, 100, 10,000 nM MG was added by spiked method. In the actual test, 0.5 mL of river water and industrial wastewater containing MG were added to 0.5 mL Ag-CDs-PBA system respectively and reacted for 10 min. At the end of the reaction, the mixture is sucked into a capillary tube for testing.

The surface morphology of the Ag-CDs was investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEM-2100) operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements of the materials was performed on a D8 9ADVANCE X-ray powder diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) with a Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 0.154056). The structural information was collected using Fourier-transform infra-red (FTIR) spectroscopy on a PE-FT spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) using a thin KBr disk as the mounting medium for the prepared materials. The surface composition and chemical state of materials was analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha). In more detail, the XPS C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s spectra were recorded and fitted separately to fully analyze the materials structure. Raman scattering measurements were recorded on an inVia Raman spectrometer (Renishaw Trading Co., Ltd., Shanghai, PCR). A Raman laser with an excitation wavelength (633 nm) was used as the excitation source. The same conditions were applied in the basal control test for the analyzed molecules collected. MG stock solutions of different concentrations were used as the molecular probes in the SERS determination experiment and the magnification of the objective lens was 50×.

The interactions and electronic transition between the CDs and PBA were analyzed using density functional theory (DFT) calculations. All quantitative calculations were optimized based on Becke’s three-parameter hybrid exchange functional and Lee–Yang–Parr correlation functional (B3LYP) with the aid of the 6-31G(d) basis set. The B3LYP method has been shown to be very reliable for studying the structural stability and spectral properties, including the composite materials of the CDs, in relevant computational studies [36,37]. The above-mentioned DFT calculations are based on the Gaussian 09 D01 software package [38]. Furthermore, the Multiwfn 3.6 program was used to obtain the transition density matrix and charge density difference, which improves the consistency between the theoretical calculations and experimental vibration frequency observed.

The morphology and chemical structure of the Ag-CDs were confirmed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 1 shows the uniform distribution of spherical particles with a dark Ag size of 40 nm as marked with boxes and light-colored CDs with diameters of 2–6 nm as marked with yellow circles. From the high magnification TEM images, most particles exhibit a well-resolved lattice fringe of 0.219 nm, which is close to the (1 0 0) plane of graphene [39–41]. In addition, the lattice spacing of 0.238 nm, which corresponds to the (1 1 1) lattice plane of face-centered cubic Ag, indicates that a large amount of plasmonic Ag was successfully obtained via the reduction of Ag. Therefore, the random grains co-existing with CDs (Fig. 1) could be considered as Ag. It is well dispersed due to the effect of the CDs on the Ag surface, improving the uniformity of the plasmonic hot spots. Furthermore, the results confirm the co-existence of Ag and C in the composite structured Ag-CDs.

The XRD patterns obtained for the Ag-CDs before and after the PBA modification step display a broad peak at 25° (0.34 nm), which confirms the existence of highly disordered carbon atoms [42] (Fig. 2 a). The four sharp peaks at 38.1, 44.3, 64.4, and 77.9° can be well indexed to the (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0) and (3 1 1) planes belonging to the face centered cubic phase of Ag (JCPDS card No. 04-0783). In the XRD analysis comparison of the Ag-CDs and Ag-CDs-PBA systems, the introduction of PBA only brings about an increase in the peak intensity illustrating the enhancement in crystallinity of Ag-CDs-PBA and stability in the active skeleton of Ag-CDs. In addition, NMR spectroscopy (1H and 13C) was used to identify the sp3-hybridized carbon atoms among the sp2-hybridized carbon atoms (Fig. S1). In the 1H NMR spectrum, the sp2 carbons, including CCH2, were observed in the range of 1–2 ppm, Ph2NH and CH at ∼2–3 ppm, N-CH3 at ∼3–4 ppm, and Ph2CH2 and PhCHCC at ∼6 ppm (Fig. S1a). In the 13C NMR spectrum, the aliphatic (sp3) carbon atoms structures were observed in the range of 30–45 ppm (Fig. S1b) [43]. Furthermore, FTIR analysis of the CDs shows more functional group information on the surface (Fig. 2b). In detail, the stretching vibrations of COH were observed at 3430 cm−1 and CH at 2923 cm−1, the stretching vibrations of NH at 3200 cm−1, bending vibrations of NH at 1570 cm−1, and the peaks at 1423 cm−1 are associated with CH in-plane bending vibrations of aromatic benzene. In addition, the asymmetric stretching vibrations of CNHC were observed at 1126 cm−1 and the vibrational absorption band of CO at 1635 cm−1 [44,45]. The contributions from the CO and OCO structures give rich binding and reaction sites for interaction with molecules.

The surface groups of the as-prepared Ag-CDs and Ag-CDs-PBA were investigated using XPS. The full survey spectrum reveals the typical peaks of C, Ag, N, and O, as shown in Fig. 2c. The Ag 3d spectrum is shown in Fig. 2d, the two strong peaks at ∼368 and ∼374 eV can be ascribed to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, and the binding energy changes indicate the role of PBA in the system. The C 1s spectrum shows four different types of carbon: Graphitic or aliphatic (CC and CC), oxygenated (CO and CO), and nitrous (OCN) [46] (Fig. 2e). The main peaks in the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2f) were observed to correspond to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively [47]. Moreover, two peaks were observed in the O 1s spectrum, as shown in Fig. 2g, indicating the existence of CO and CO, respectively. It is worth noting that all of these results further confirm that the strong and unique π-π effect of the CDs and PBA causes significant changes in the functional groups after the introduction of PBA in the Ag-CDs system, especially the significant reduction in the number of OCO/OCN and CO groups, upon comparing the Ag-CDs and Ag-CDs-PBA systems. These results are listed in Table S1. The Ag 3d content for the substrate modified by PBA was larger than the sample without PBA, revealing the indirect interactions formed between the CDs and PBA. Moreover, the percentage of functional elements (C, N, and O) was low in the Ag-CDs-PBA composite system when compared to the corresponding structure of the Ag-CDs support, which indicates that the interactions formed between the large number of amino groups in the CDs and boric acid groups of PBA result in a uniquely stable conjugated system. Since their roles are beneficial for CDs fluorescence quenching, Ag-CDs-PBA should exhibit enhanced performance for target molecule detection when compared to Ag-CDs.

It has been shown that PBA has a high binding and CDs further lead to strong interactions based on Ag in the Ag-CDs-PBA system, which are capable of greatly improving the SERS enhancement ability for detection. The Raman spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 3 a) shows no obvious D or G bands with a low carbon lattice structure content. The characteristic G band and D bands were detected under the action of Ag (Fig. 3b). Under laser irradiation, the collective oscillation of the free electrons occurs on the Ag surface, so there will be enhancement of the local electromagnetic field [48,49]. And Raman spectroscopy can be used to effectively explain the chemical structure of carbon-based materials. Nevertheless, the addition of Ag cannot completely eliminate the strong fluorescence of the CDs and the Raman signal is still weak. However, due to the unique strong effect between PBA and the CDs, it can be clearly seen that the D band (1343 cm−1), the G band (1587 cm−1), in plane deformation vibrations of the OCN structure (617 cm−1), and symmetric vibrations of the CN bonds (778 cm−1) of the CDs via the CT effect in the Ag-CDs-PBA hybrid (Fig. 3c), which can be attributed to the fluorescence being quenched in the CDs within the tight π-π stacking structure.

As one of the perfect graphene materials, the CDs in this SERS substrate have a high degree of defects, which act as a bridge to gain or lose electrons promoting the CT effect during the catalytic reaction [50,51]. In addition, the efficient detection performance was shown to recognize MG molecules when compared to the systems before and after the PBA modification step. The SERS spectra of MG on Ag-CDs and Ag-CDs-PBA were recorded (Fig. 3d) and various characteristic bands were detected. The relatively strong SERS band observed at 1617 cm−1 was assigned to the in-plane vibrations of the ring CC bonds, the peak at 1368 cm−1 was attributed to the N-phenyl stretching, at 1173 cm−1 was caused by in-plane modes of CH bending[52], the peaks at 1221 and 910 cm−1 were derived from CH rocking and CH out-of-plane bending, respectively [53], and that at 801 cm−1 was matched to the ring CH out-of-plane bending. It is worth noting that the higher intensity observed for each characteristic Raman peak of the MG molecule in the Ag-CDs-PBA structure when compared to the Ag-CDs system. This result fully illustrates the key signal enhancement brought by PBA in molecular detection. On the other hand, when Ag was used as SERS substrate to perform the Raman test on MG molecules, the MG enhancement signal obtained was much weaker than that caused by Ag-CDs, which confirmed the promoting role of CDs in the composite system (Fig. S2). Moreover, structural information on the MB molecule was clearly identified, revealing the universal performance of Ag-CDs-PBA as a substrate during the SERS study (Fig. S3). The Raman characteristic bands observed at 1622, 1526, 1180, and 589 cm−1 can be attributed to the benzene ring stretching, NH2, CH, and C-S-C deformation of the MB molecules.

To prove that this excellent performance was due to Ag-CDs-PBA, the change in the SERS spectrum of MG with various concentrations was studied. Fig. 3e shows this enhanced signal was observed in the fabricated Ag-CDs-PBA under the concentration effect. It can be found that the intensity of each characteristic peak reduces upon decreasing the concentration of MG. However, the characteristic signal can be observed at MG concentrations of <10–10 M, which reveals that the Ag-CDs-PBA SERS substrate exhibits high sensitivity in molecular significantly. The formation of high-density hot spots causes an enhancement in the local field (ELoc) and large number of amplification of the SERS signal. It is also notable that the SERS spectra obtained for MG still have the same shape with those at a low concentration of 10–9 and 10–10 M. This behavior shows the same orientation of the MG molecules at different concentrations by the plasmonic substrate. The good linear response shows a high regression coefficient of 0.997 for the logarithm of the SERS intensity versus the concentration of MG (log (C)) in the range of 10−4–10−10 M (Fig. 3f). This is an accurate fitting relationship, which was confirmed to follow the Langmuir isotherm for the adsorption of the target molecules [54]. Furthermore, compared with the other substrates reported previously in SERS detection (Table 1 ) [3,55–61], the result has outstanding advantages in linear range and sensitivity.

In addition, the uniformity and reproducibility are key factors for SERS detection, which can be evaluated by randomly selecting multiple locations on the substrate and collecting their corresponding SERS spectra for MG molecules (Fig. 4 a). It can be found that there was no obvious difference among the spectral peaks, which confirms that this substrate has good reproducibility and uniformity. These SERS spectra have clear characteristic peaks without the interference of noise peaks. Furthermore, the RSD values of the vibrations at 1617, 1173, and 801 cm−1 were calculated to be 5.77, 7.11, and 5.79 % of MG from the different points on the fabricated SERS substrate, respectively (Fig. 4b).

Consequently, the application of the Ag-CDs-PBA nanohybrid is extremely feasible and effective for the sensitive and quantitative detection of MG. At the same time, this result highlights the excellent properties of the plasmonic material more accurately. Fig. S4 shows the sensitivity further implemented by MB molecules was as high as 10−9 M. The SERS intensity of the characteristic NH2 band has a good linear relationship with the concentration in the range from 10−4 to 10−9 M. In addition, the neat peaks at different points and corresponding accurate RSD values again demonstrate the excellent uniform performance. This indicates that this substrate can be used as an effective detection platform even in real samples for the quantitative analysis of variousmolecules. Importantly, this PBA modulated Ag-CDs as a SERS substrate has long term stability and the SERS spectrum can be collected after 3 months storage under an ambient environment (Fig. S5). Obviously, the intensity and contour of the characteristic signal of MG do not significantly change even the SERS nanostructure placed for a long time. Therefore, the novel Ag-CDs-PBA composite is endowed with excellent stability and high activity for detecting molecules. Accordingly, the sufficient active vibration sites and appropriate plasmonic coupling were not lost in this stable structure and can be used to amplify the SERS signal.

To further realize the major breakthrough of Ag-CDs-PBA in the field of actual environmental monitoring, the recognition ability of target molecules in mixtures with other polluting molecules is essential for the SERS detection. First, the SERS spectrum of R6G (Fig. 4c), CV (Fig. 4d), and RB (Fig. 4e) were analyzed at different concentrations to obtain the higher sensitivity of various molecules on the substrate Ag-CDs-PBA, and further its universal detection was expanded. It can be found that the characteristic peak can still be recognized when the concentration of R6G molecule is 10−10 M, the CCC ring in-plane and out-of-plane bending and CH vibrations at 611, 772 cm−1, and the main bands associated with CC stretching vibrations at 1190, 1356, 1506, and 1643 cm−1. The CV molecule detection revealed the sensitivity of 10–8 M, but it is inferred that the basic ring skeletal vibration, CH bending, and ring CC stretching observed much lower than this concentration. And the sensitivity of the RB molecule tested is 10−9 M, which has a general trend that the increased concentration will enhance the molecular signal. Therefore, it is clarified that the excellent application of the constructed SERS subtract for trace identification of multiple molecules. In addition, the complicate system is composed of a variety of sample matrices explored (Fig. 4f). The results show that MG molecule can be recognized in the same kind of binary mixed systems, and the SERS spectra of MG molecule itself can still be found in situ monitoring of multi-component components. The analysis results suggest that Ag-CDs-PBA structure has broad development prospects to achieve toxic organic molecular identification in more complex environments.

It has been confirmed that Ag-CDs-PBA as a SERS substrate used for MG detection has high sensitivity. In this system, the enhanced Raman signal of the Ag-CDs-PBA active nanomaterials can be attributed to the plasmonic Ag and synergistic effect of the three components based on strong charge transfer. First, based on the presence of CDs in the large area, on the one hand a large amount of Ag was uniformly dispersed to expand the area of plasmon hotspots, and on the other hand Raman enhancement caused by small areas with <50 nm of scattering Ag dimers. In addition, the strongest SPR effect of the Ag was expanded and introduced into CDs and PBA in the complex system. The CDs and PBA are excited to produce the electrons-hole pairs in the appropriate optical band gap. In CDs-PBA system, the potential difference between the LUMO based PBA and CDs allows the PBA to act as an electron donor to transfer electrons to the CDs to quench their fluorescence, and the close spatial orientation between the amine groups of the CDs and boronic acid groups of PBA forming π-π stacking interactions. Moreover, the reaction leading to a decrease in the amine groups on the CDs will be more conducive to the quenching of the fluorescence. This important discovery was confirmed using DFT calculations, which showed that there is a strong interaction between the CDs and adsorbed PBA. The varying degree of PBA is supported by a large number of holes in the boric acid group and high-density electrons in the functional groups of the CDs (Fig. 5 a). In addition, the transition density of the CDs-PBA complex shows the equally pronounced conclusion and makes it very clear that this is strong electron-hole coherence, which further provides sufficient evidence for this excitation in principle with charge redistributions within each molecule (Fig. 5b). Some of the electrons in the LUMO of the CDs as bridges can also be trapped by the Ag involving a rapid transfer process from the excited state to the orbital of Ag and further dynamic queuing fluorescence. To sum up, it can be determined that the construction of Ag-CDs-PBA possessing high-density hotspots [62] significantly improves the SERS activity induced by the collective oscillations of the conduction-band and outstanding photo-generated electrons transfer characteristics of the CDs bridge constructed. Therefore, more analytes can be absorbed on the substrate to form a significant SERS effect during detection.

The SERS performance of the substrate also depends on the time of photoirradiation. It can be seen that the signal peak intensity of the MG molecules shows an increasing trend with time (Fig. S6a). In addition, the in-plane vibrations of the ring CC and out-of-plane bending of C–H vibration intensity intuitively increase under continuous light irradiation (Fig. S6b). Electron migration can be promoted through the CDs acting as the bridge over a long time, which would greatly benefit the interfacial CT and reduce the recombination efficiency of the electric-hole pairs. Finally, it will accelerate the dynamic quenching of the CDs. Furthermore, the density of Ag increases significantly leading to an expansion of the active vibration sites reaching the distance-controlled plasmonic coupling in contact with the inter-particles [63–66]. The cumulative measurement can pave the way for an ideal experimental test. This enhanced illumination also facilitates the SERS activity obtained during process monitoring (Fig. S6c). This highlights the strong signal of the molecules under high power illumination, whether from the color observation or the corresponding curve trend (Fig. S6d). Multiple accumulations further modify the SERS spectrum and also result in the enhancement of the characteristic signal. The noise signal is largely eliminated and the detection accuracy improved with an increase in the accumulation time (Fig. S6e). It was also found that in the two-dimensional signal graph, the effect of three times accumulation is obvious with strong regional signals, which shows the intensity difference of the various characteristic bands (Fig. S6f). Such a particular structure shows that as light traps, photons are more easily excited and have greater opportunities to come in contact with the Ag surface and the accelerated CT results in a significant SERS effect.

The analysis of MG with lower concentration can be realized under lower energy irradiation. Nevertheless, a breakthrough bottleneck of some designed SERS substrates are their relatively weak SERS performance, which subsequently suffers from limitation in the development of practical application. To elucidate the promising effects of Ag-CDs-PBA, the SERS activity by the target MG was estimated through the analytical enhancement factor (AEF) following the standard equation:
(1)
AEF =
I SERS
/
C SERS
/
I O
/
C O
where ISERS and Io is the corresponding intensities of MG on Ag-CDs-PBA and in the reference solution, respectively. The CSERS selected is the lowest concentration detected at 10−10 M and the CO values of 10−2 M aqueous solution of this MG. To calculate the AEF, the SERS spectra of the MG molecules on the Ag-CDs-PBA substrate (curve a) and the reference solution (curve b) was presented in Fig. 5c. The resulting AEF was calculated as 4.04 × 107 at band 801 cm−1 and 5.39 × 107 with a characteristic peak of 1617 cm−1. According to the study, it was analyzed that the feasible inspection of MG in actual samples was constructed by Ag-CDs-PBA.

As we all know, MG has been used worldwide, but it inevitably causes harm to human health. The direct damage of fish species living in water can be caused by MG. It is extremely urgent to use substrate Ag-CDs-PBA to detect MG in real time. Fish samples soaked with MG (10−7 M) were tested with Ag-CDs-PBA substrate, as shown in Fig. 5d. The MG characteristic peaks are identified and mild energy is given to prevent damage to the real sample. The results showed that the construction of Ag-CDs-PBA system could effectively monitor the presence of toxins in food under SERS technology.

SERS substrates used in commercial applications should also comply with the principle of recyclability for the corresponding activity levels required. Further cost savings can be achieved if the substrate exhibits the self-cleaning properties of the assay. As shown in Fig. 5e, the enhanced MG molecules detected after the cleaning process are broken down under visible light irradiation, and the same concentration of MG is attached to the substrate, the SERS signal is reproduced surprisingly. In this iteration, it can be observed that the SERS signal of MG disappears almost completely (curves 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10) and its characteristic Raman spectrum is presented again (curves 1, 3, 5, 7 and 9) correspondingly. In Fig. 5f, it can be visualised that this material still has the SERS activity required for qualitative detection even after 5 times of recycling, and the slight reduction in SERS signal intensity is justified by the decrease in adsorption as well as hot spots. This excellent performance with multiple recycling shows promise in a competitive edge in the ranks of practical production applications.

In summary, a facile and high-active SERS substrate has been successfully fabricated meeting the requirements of low cost and flexibility. The developed SERS substrate realizes the highly sensitive and selective identification MG, which has potential for the simultaneous detection of multiple chemical and biological analytes in mixture. The CDs with functional structures were regulated by PBA to impede the return of electrons to the ground state, quenching the fluorescence through the formation of strong CT and plasmonic Ag to further enhance the electron flow driven by the synergistic effect, amplifying the molecular signal. The specific identification realized for the target molecule plays a leading role in anti-interference analysis. The chemical structure and intrinsic enhancement mechanism have been revealed in detail including the electron flow direction and apparent plasmonic effect on the surface of the Ag-CDs-PBA hybrid, demonstrating its promising value in the environmental monitoring field. More meaningfully, practical detection of MG can be obtained even at trace levels in a complex environment. This novel study can provide valuable reference toward the design of functional materials used for electronic transmission and analyte detection.
All Institutional and National Guidelines for the care and use of animals (fisheries) were followed.

Yanqiu Yang: Methodology, Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Lingru Kong: Data curation. Yong Ding: Software. Lixin Xia: Data curation. Shuo Cao: Data curation. Peng Song: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 11974152), the Science Program of Liaoning Provincial Department of Education (LJKZ0097), the Intercollegiate cooperation project of colleges and universities of Liaoning Provincial Department of Education, Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC2002097), Liaoning Provincial Natural Science Foundation (2020-YKLH-22).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jare.2024.02.004.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary material to this article:
Supplementary data 1
